messages.content: One of the first examples of DGA based extractants for the 4f-block (Ln) elements was reported by Stephan et al.[Citation14] Using N,N’-dimethyl-N,N’-dioctyldigylcolamide (DMDODGA) they studied a range of metal ions and their distribution ratios, in extraction from HNO3 buffer at pH = 5.2. The highest distribution ratios were observed with Yb and La with Sr and Ca being the next most extracted elements. The lack of study of pH dependence of the distribution ratio means that the true potential of the DGA molecules for the extraction of Ln (and An) is not realized in these early reports. Further work by Guoxin et al. studied the acid extraction properties and the Ln extraction dependence on acid concentration and temperature, filling in some of the gaps.[Citation27] TODGA was developed by Sasaki et al. in Japan.[Citation18] The amidic octyl chains increase the solubility in hydrocarbon diluents, which are commonly employed as the organic diluents in biphasic nuclear waste separations. The group synthesized multiple DGA molecules and found that the longer the amidic alkyl chain the lower the Am3+ and Eu3+ distribution ratios but the greater the solubility in organic diluents. They also found that the distribution ratios increased with increasing HNO3 concentration for all An in all oxidation states up to a value of 6 M HNO3 (the highest they measured). Slope analysis of the extraction data demonstrated that the M:L stoichiometry of complexation was between 1:3 and 1:4 for Th4+, UO22+, Pu4+, Am3+ and Cm3+. Due to the size of the DGAs (and the shape of UO22+) it is unlikely that these are all inner sphere processes and more likely a combined inner and outer sphere association with micelle formation operates (see Section 2.2).[Citation28] Distribution ratios were greater for the heavier Ln and An, with the Ln being more readily extracted than the An in n-dodecane (see Table S7).

The extraction of 75 elements by N,N,N ,N -tetraoctyl-3-oxapentanediamide (TODGA) from nitric acid to n-dodecane was investigated and discussed concerning distribution ratios (D) of metal ions and their ionic radii. The extractability for elements depends strongly on their oxidation state. Monovalent and pentavalent ions were not extractable. In divalent metal extraction, Ca(II) (ionic radius: 100 pm) showed the highest D-value and the D decreased with increase or decrease of the ionic radius. Trivalent and tetravalent ions with ionic radii of 87–113 and 83–94 pm, respectively, showed the D over 1000, and the extracted chemical forms were determined to be M(TODGA)3(NO3)n or M(TODGA)4(NO3)n (n = 3 or 4). The oxonium ions with higher oxidation states than tetravalent one forms the complicated metal-complex with TODGA, and the number of TODGA associated in those extraction reactions was confirmed to be two at most.

For the reprocessing of spent nuclear fuel and the partitioning of high-level radioactive liquid waste, many extractants have been developed, investigated and employed up to now. Examples are tributylphosphate (TBP) for extraction of U(VI) and Pu(IV) in PUREX process, octyl(phenyl)- N,N-diisobutylcarbamoylmethylphosphine oxide (O D(iB) CMPO), dimethyldibutyltetradecylmalonamide (DMDBTDMA) and trialkylphosphine oxide (TRPO) [1–3] for extraction of actinides (An), cobalt(III) dicarbollide and crown ether derivatives for extraction of Cs(I) and Sr(II) [4–8] and Cyanex 301, bis-triazinyl-pyridine (BTP) and 2,4,6-tris-2- pyridyl-1,3,5-triazine (TPTZ) for the extraction and separation of An(III) from lanthanides (Ln(III)) [9–14] have been proposed in this field.

Recently, based on the earlier studies [15–17], N,N,N ,N - tetraoctyl-3-oxapentanediamide (TODGA) was synthesized and studied in our laboratory [18–21]. TODGA is a tridentate ligand, well-soluble in aliphatic n-dodecane and shows very high distribution ratios for An(III) and An(IV) from concentrated nitric acid to n-dodecane. The extraction of An and fission products by TODGA was reported [22]. TODGA is a novel extractant, therefore it is important to know the extraction behavior of all elements and to expand the utility of TODGA. The representative extractants, e.g., TBP and trioctylphosphine oxide (TOPO), were employed widely and their basic data for the extraction of all elements were summarized [23].

In this paper, we have investigated 75 elements extraction by TODGA. The extracted species for the elements and the relationship between the distribution ratios and their ionic radii are discussed.

The extractant, N,N,N ,N -tetraoctyl-3-oxapentanediamide (98% pure) was produced by Kanto Chemical Co. Inc. Nitric acid, n-dodecane and other chemical reagents were of analytical grade. All metal ions for the extraction experiments were supplied from the standard solutions for atomic absorption spectrometry (Wako Pure Chemical Industries Ltd.). The oxidation states in these standard solutions were considered to be maintained.

Certain concentration (0.002–6.0M (mol/dm3)) of nitric acid containing 1 or 100 ppm metal ions was used as an aqueous phase and certain concentrations (0.001–0.5 M) of TODGA/n-dodecane after pre-equilibration was used as an organic phase. The same volumes (0.5–1.0 ml) of both phases were mixed and shaken mechanically with 1500 rpm for 50 min at 25 ℃.

After centrifugation, 0.4 ml of the aqueous phase was taken and diluted to 5ml (0.5M HNO3) using nitric acid and distilled water. For the organic phase, 0.4 ml was taken and the ions were stripped from the samples twice using 1ml of diluted nitric acid (0.01 M) for Ca(II), Sr(II), Cd(II), In(II), Pb(II), Ba(II) and Bi(III), 0.2Moxalic acid in 2MHNO3 for Zr(IV) and Hf(IV), or 0.5MHCI for Sn(IV), Sb(III), Re(VII), Os(VIII), Ir(III), Pt(IV), Hg(II) and Bi(III). Concerning the backward extraction, the temperature was controlled at 35 ◦C in order to accelerate the metal-stripping. For lanthanides (Ln(III)), Sc(III), Y(III) and alkaline earth metals, the organic phase was decomposed by HClO4 +HNO3 instead of the stripping extraction, and the residue was dissolved in 0.5M HNO3. For other elements, the sample solutions from the organic phase were not prepared for the measurement and the concentration in the organic phase was calculated from the difference of the concentration in the aqueous phase before and after extraction.

Fig. 1 shows the D-values of the metal ions examined as a function of the nitric acid concentration for extraction by 0.1M TODGA/n-dodecane. In this figure, monovalent: 7, divalent: 14, trivalent: 31, tetravalent: 11, pentavalent: 5, hexavalent: 4, heptavalent: 2 and octavalent: 1, total 75 ions were illustrated. Some of the radioactive elements, halogen and rare gases were not examined in this work. The monovalent ions, e.g., alkali metals, Ag(I), Tl(I) and pentavalent ions of V(V), Nb(V) and Ta(V) have small extraction. However, some of the divalent, trivalent and tetravalent ions have high D-values, especially for the metals in IIa, IIIa, and IVa groups. The extraction behavior of the ions varies greatly when their oxidation states are different, so that D-values should be compared for the same oxidation states.

From the D-values in Fig. 1, the elements examined were classified into four groups: (1) high distribution ratios with DM above 10, (2) intermediate distribution ratios with DM within 1–10, (3) low distribution ratios with DM in the range of 0.01–1 and (4) unextractable ions when they were not detectable in the organic phase. The ions in each group are as follows: (1) Ca(II), Sc(III), Y(III), Zr(IV), In(III), Ln(III), Hf(IV), W(VI), Au(III), Hg(II), Bi(III), Th(IV), U(VI) and Pu(IV); (2) Cr(VI), Sr(II), Tc(VII), Pd(II), Sb(III), Re(VII), Os(VIII), Ir(III) and Pb(II); (3) Mo(VI), Ru(III), Cd(II), Sn(IV), Ba(II), Pt(IV) and Np(V); (4) alkali metals, Be(II), B(III), Mg(II), Al(III), Si(IV), Ti(IV), V(V), Mn(II), Fe(III), Co(II), Ni(II), Cu(II), Zn(II), Ga(III), Ge(IV), As(III), Se(IV), Nb(V), Rh(III), Ag(I), Te(IV), Ta(V) and Tl(I).

As shown in Fig. 1, some of the divalent metal ions were extractable by TODGA; the subsequent study is to investigate the extraction reactions of divalent metals and discuss the extraction behavior. Slope analysis can provide the useful information on the composition of the extracted chemical species. The D-values of divalent ions against the concentration of TODGA in n-dodecane are plotted in Fig. 2. From these results, the slopes obtained were: Ca(II), 2.4; Sr(II), 2.2; Ba(II), 2.0; Cd(II), 2.0; Pb(II), 2.2; Pd(II), 2.2; Hg(II), 0.85. The slope analyses indicate that the molar ratios of the extracted species are mainly 1:2 for metal TODGA. On the contrary, TODGA is presumably involved in Hg(II) complexation and the coordination number of Hg(II) may exhibit four [24]. Metal ions with low coordination number leads to low associated number of TODGA in the extraction reaction and it is difficult to extract such a metal complex into an aliphatic solvent like n-dodecane. Followed by this consideration, the divalent metals with coordination number four, e.g., Be(II), Cu(II), Zn(II) and Cd(II), are unextractable (Fig. 1).

The plot of D against ionic radii of all divalent metal ions is shown in Fig. 3, using the ionic radii of Shannon [24]. In Fig. 3, the coordination number of metals is four for Cu, Zn, Cd and Hg, and six for Mg, Ca, Mn, Co, Ni, Sr, Ba and Pb. The divalent ions, Pd(II), Hg(II), Ca(II), Sr(II) and Pb(II) with 86–119 pm, have D-value >1 while the unextractable metals have ionic radii lower than 75 pm. The maximum D was obtained by Ca (ionic radius; 100 pm). It is noted that the D decreases with increase or decrease of ionic radius from that of Ca, which suggests that around 100 pm of ionic radius for divalent ions is the most suitable size for complexation with TODGA.

The extraction behavior of 31 trivalent ions by TODGA was shown in Fig. 1. It was found that the elements in IIIa group (Sc(III), Y(III), Ln(III) and An(III)) and Bi(III) have high D-values and can be extracted quantitatively by 0.1M TODGA/n-dodecane from 3M HNO3. The distribution ratios of extractable trivalent ions are plotted against TODGA concentration in Fig. 4 and those of lanthanides, in Fig. 5. Slope analyses give the values: Sc(III), 2.6; Y(III), 3.0; Ru(III), 0.59; In(III), 2.5; Sb(III), 1.3; La(III), 3.2; Ce(III), 3.7; Pr(III), 3.6; Nd(III), 3.4; Sm(III), 4.0; Eu(III), 3.9; Gd(III), 4.0; Tb(III), 4.2; Dy(III), 4.0; Ho(III), 4.2; Er(III), 4.2; Tm(III), 4.2; Yb(III), 4.1; Lu(III), 4.2; Bi(III), 2.7; Am(III), 3.4; Cf(III), 3.3. These values were mostly between three and four, which suggests the extracted species to be M(TODGA)3(NO3)3 or M(TODGA)4(NO3)3. Ru(III)ion forms the nitrosyl complexes, its coordination sites are probably already occupied by NO donors. For Ln series, it was found that the DM values gradually increase from the light lanthanides to the heavier ones, which follows the trend in the charge density [18].

The plots of D against ionic radii of trivalent metal ions are shown in Fig. 6. The coordination number of metals as given in ref. [24] is six (Al, Fe, As, Ru, Rh, In, Sb, Ir, Au and Bi) and eight (Sc, Y, Lns and Ans). D increase somewhat with the increase of ionic radii from 70 to 87 pm, and D for the ions with ionic radii between 87 and 113 pm, such as Sc, Y, lanthanides, actinides and Bi, are higher than 1000. The highest D was observed in Lu(III) (ionic radius: 97.7 pm), which has a similar radius to that of Ca(II).

When the concentration of nitric acid is greater than 1M, Zr(IV), Hf(IV) and An(IV) can be extracted quantitatively by 0.1M TODGA/n-dodecane but other tetravalent cations are weakly extracted, as shown in Fig. 1. The relation of D values with TODGA concentrations are shown in Fig. 7. The slopes, 3.4, 3.1, 3.1 and 3.0 for Zr(IV), Hf(IV), Th(IV) and Pu(IV), respectively, were approximately 3, and slopes of the other metals: Pt(IV) and Sn(IV), were 2.0 and 0.64. It is wellknown that Zr(IV) and Hf(IV), whose ionic radii are 84 and 83 pm, have very similar chemical behavior. For the same experimental conditions, the distribution ratios of Zr(IV) are higher than those of Hf(IV), showing high separation factors (SF) of about 7.5. Here, SF is defined as D(Zr)/D(Hf).

The plots of D against ionic radii of tetravalent metal ions are shown in Fig. 8. The coordination number of metals referred in this paper [24] is six (Ti, Se, Sn, Te and Pt) and eight (Zr, Hf, Th and Pu). The ionic radii of Hf(IV), Zr(IV), Pu(IV) and Th(IV), which have D-values higher than 1000, were in the range between 83 and 105 pm. It is noted that theD-values of metals with ionic radii smaller than 70 pm are negligible, except Pt(IV), and those with ionic radii larger than 83 pm are extremely high, which suggest that there is a strong size effect on tetravalent metal-complexation with TODGA. The highest D was observed for Zr(IV), which exhibits a small ionic radius compared to Ca(II) and Lu(III).

We have investigated 75 ions extraction behavior by TODGAfrom nitric acid into n-dodecane. Among these ions, the monovalent metals, divalent ions with ionic radii smaller than 80 pm, trivalent ions with ionic radii smaller than 70 pm, tetravalent ions with ionic radii smaller than 60 pm and pentavalent ions, were weakly extracted by TODGA from nitric acid. The strongly extracted ions with D higher than 50 are Ca(II), Sc(III), Y(III), Zr(IV), Ln(III), Hf(IV), Bi(III), An(III) and An(IV) whose ionic radii are 100 pm for divalent, 87–116 pm for trivalent and 83–105 pm for tetravalent. From the slope analyses, itwas confirmed that these metal ions in ndodecane form the chemical species M(TODGA)3(NO3)n or M(TODGA)4(NO3)n (n = 3 or 4), except Ca(II) which forms (Ca(TODGA)2(NO3)2).

Advanced solvent extraction processes, namely DIAMEX, SANEX or GANEX, for the separation of the minor actinides (americium and curium) are under development within Europe. The tridentate diglycolamide ligand, TODGA, shows many interesting properties and is under investigation in conjunction with a variety of other extractants for the DIAMEX and SANEX processes as well as the GANEX process. In order to successfully demonstrate these processes, understanding the acid extraction into the organic phases is critical to process flowsheet design and modelling. Here nitric acid extractions into TODGA have been measured and models produced using an equilibrium based approach accounting for nitric acid activities in the aqueous phase.

In the heterogeneous route, a number of processes have been proposed that separate the minor actinides (MAs) from the highly active raffinate (HAR) stream after the PUREX process. In Europe these processes have been termed DIAMEX and SANEX.[4-6] An alternative concept under investigation within Europe is the Grouped ActiNide EXtraction process (GANEX) in which all of the actinides are routed together.[7-9] Prospective extractants are diglycolamide (DGA) ligands which have proved very successful in DIAMEX and SANEX process development[10-12] and show many ideal properties for grouped actinide extractions.[13, 14]

The tridentate ligand TODGA (N,N,N’,N’- tetraoctyl diglycolamide) is the leading example of this class of ligands.[15-19] Indeed, a number of DIAMEX-style flowsheet trials have been performed with 0.2 M TODGA / 0.5 M TBP (tri-n-butyl-phosphate) in an alkane diluent, successfully demonstrating the complete co-extraction of trivalent lanthanides and minor actinides.[10-12] An improved formulation (0.2 M TODGA / 5 % octanol) has been proposed and tested for its use in an innovative(i)-SANEX process aimed at separating the trivalent actinides from the lanthanides.[20] 0.005 M TODGA has also been used as a phase modifier with the CyMe4BTBP (6,6’-bis(5,5,8,8-tetramethyl-5,6,7,8-tetrahydrobenzo[1,2,4]-triazin-3-yl)-[2,2’]-bipyridine) ligand to selectively extract trivalent actinides from a simulated HAR in a 1-cycle SANEX process.[21] Recently, a system containing TODGA and the malonamide, DMDOHEMA (N,N‘-dimethyl-N,N‘-dioctylhexylethoxymalonamide) has been shown to have favourable properties for applications in a GANEX process.[13]

Using a simple concentration based model, Mowafy[22] found that the 1:1 HNO3:TODGA complex was the extracted species at 1 M HNO3 with a conditional equilibrium constant KH = 0.27 ±0.04 in benzene diluent. Guoxin[23] reported HNO3 extraction into a related DGA molecule N,N’-dimethyl-N,N’-dicotyl-3- oxadiglycolamide (DMDODGA). Using the same approach as Mowafy, they also find 1:1 HNO3:DMDODGA complexation from extractions at 2 M HNO3 with a conditional equilibrium constant KH = 0.38 ±0.04 in chloroform diluent. Ansari[15] confirms 1:1 complexation with TODGA in n-dodecane (aqueous phase of 1 M HNO3) with a conditional equilibrium constant KH = 4.1 ±0.4. Sasaki finds HNO3 extraction varies with diluent with the apparent solvation number around 1 for dodecane[24, 25]. Modolo[10], however, shows that the apparent solvation number varies with TODGA concentration, from (HNO3)2TODGA at 6 M HNO3 and 0.1 M TODGA to (HNO3)1.5TODGA with 0.6 M TODGA in TPH (tetrahydrogenated propylene) and dodecane diluents. In extractions in TPH with 5 % octanol, Geist[20] also proposes (HNO3)2TODGA species must be present above 3 M HNO3 since the extracted nitric acid exceeds the TODGA concentration, although he notes the 1:1 complex is the dominant species.

The use of activity coefficients in the modeling of solvent extraction systems has been reported by a number of authors. HNO3 extraction into TBP has been studied extensively[26-36] and several models using activity coefficients have been reported.[27, 37] Chaiko and Vandegrift calculated molal activity coefficients using a method by Bromley[38] before converting them into molar units[37] while Davis recalculated in molar units molal activity coefficients produced from freezing point data.[27] Both then looked at the extraction of a number of complexes into the organic solution. Models for extraction of HNO3 into DMDOHEMA[39] and octanol[40] have been reported, again using activity coefficients but calculated using Specific Interaction Theory.[41].

Experiments have been carried out to measure nitric acid extraction into TODGA and the results used to produce models of this extraction based on an equilibrium approach, accounting for nitric acid activities in the aqueous phase. Future work will extend the modelling to mixed extractant systems of relevance to prospective advanced solvent extraction processes (e.g. TODGA plus DMDOHEMA).

The above method was used to fit values of the equilibrium constants to NNL data on the acid extraction into solutions of TODGA in odourless kerosene diluent and the calculated values for organic acid compared with experimental values. The equilibrium constants calculated are shown in Table 1 (Case 1). As can be seen from Figure 1 (Case 1) the calculated values for the extracted acid are much lower at the highest acidities than the experimental values. Studying the experimental data reveals that the concentration of extracted acid at the highest acidities is more than twice the concentration of TODGA present in solution. This indicates that complexes containing more than two acid molecules to one TODGA must be formed at high acidity.

To explore further complexes the 4:1 complex (i.e. x = 4 in Equation (6)) was then added to the system and while the resulting value for the equilibrium constant for this complex was low it did improve the fit over Case 2. The value of the equilibrium constant ß12 produced in this case was also very low (1x10-8) and so it was decided to remove this complex from the system to assess its impact. Thus the equilibrium constants were recalculated for the system using equations (1), (3) and (6) with x = 3 and x = 4 (Table 1 Case 3). The results are shown in Figure 1 (Case 3). Indeed, simplistic plots of log DHNO3 against log[TODGA] give gradients close to 1 (1.09, 1.02 and 1.00 for 1, 3 and 6 M HNO3 respectively) indicating that only one TODGA is present in the extracted complexes. This is in agreement with the literature where 1:1 and 2:1 complexes have been reported but no evidence has been found for 1:2 complexes. [10, 15, 20, 22-25] Most published modeling approaches and experimental data for systems of this kind restrict the acidity of the solutions to a lower range than those considered here and as can be seen in Figure 1 the extracted acid can be reasonably well modelled at lower acidities without the need for complexes containing more than two HNO3 molecules.

TODGA is known to self-aggregate in solution[15, 44-46] and the strength of these aggregates will have an impact on the ability of the extractant to form complexes with other species. The effect of the aggregates may be to reduce the available “free” extractant concentration. Alternatively, the aggregates may themselves extract species from the aqueous phase forming large complexes which may average out as those complexes which have been modelled here. Further studies of the solvent systems are necessary to ascertain how aggregation effects observed spectroscopically affect HNO3 extraction equilibria.

Using activity based calculations (at least for the aqueous phase) thermodynamically based models for HNO3 extraction into TODGA –odourless kerosene solvents can be developed and information obtained on the likely complexes formed. While there are a number of issues that ideally would be addressed to improve the modelling, it is clear that good estimates can be made of HNO3 extraction in this system simply by accounting for nitric acid activity in the aqueous phase.

Over the last decade there has been much interest in the applications of diglycolamide (DGA) ligands for the extraction of the trivalent lanthanide and actinide ions from PUREX high active raffinates or dissolved spent nuclear fuel. Of the DGAs, the N,N,N’,N’-tetraoctyldiglycolamide (TODGA) is the best known and most widely studied. A number of new actinide separation processes have been proposed based on extraction with TODGA. This review covers TODGAbased processes and extraction data, specifically focusing on how phase modifiers have been used to increase metal loading and thus enhance the operating process envelopes. Effects of third phase formation and the organic phase speciation are reviewed in this context. Relevant aspects of the extraction chemistry of important solvents (TODGA-modifier-diluent combinations) are described and their performances demonstrated by a consideration of the published flowsheet tests. It is seen that modifiers are successfully enabling the use of TODGA in actinide separation processes but to date the identification and testing of suitable modifiers has been rather empirical. There is a growing understanding of the fundamental chemistry occurring in the organic phase and how that affects extractant speciation andmetal loading capacity but studies are still needed if TODGA-based flowsheets are to become an industrially deployable option for minor actinide (MA) recovery processes.

One of the first examples of DGA based extractants for the 4f-block (Ln) elements was reported by Stephan et al.[14] Using N,N’-dimethyl-N,N’-dioctyldigylcolamide (DMDODGA) they studied a range of metal ions and their distribution ratios, in extraction from HNO3 buffer at pH = 5.2. The highest distribution ratios were observed with Yb and La with Sr and Ca being the next most extracted elements. The lack of study of pH dependence of the distribution ratio means that the true potential of the DGA molecules for the extraction of Ln (and An) is not realized in these early reports.

The group synthesized multiple DGA molecules and found that the longer the amidic alkyl chain the lower the Am3+ and Eu3+ distribution ratios but the greater the solubility in organic diluents. They also found that the distribution ratios increased with increasing HNO3 concentration for all An in all oxidation states up to a value of 6 M HNO3 (the highest they measured). Slope analysis of the extraction data demonstrated that the M:L stoichiometry of complexation was between 1:3 and 1:4 for Th4+, UO22+, Pu4+, Am3+ and Cm3+. Due to the size of the DGAs (and the shape of UO2 2+) it is unlikely that these are all inner sphere processes and more likely a combined inner and outer sphere association with micelle formation operates (see Section 2.2).[28] Distribution ratios were greater for the heavier Ln and An, with the Ln being more readily extracted than the An in n-dodecane (see Table S7).

Mowafy and Aly investigated the post-separation speciation of Sr2+, Eu3+, and Am3+ with TODGA in both benzene and n-dodecane solution.[30] Through measurement of distribution ratios with varying TODGA concentrations they proposed that speciation is dominated by Sr(TODGA)3(NO3)2, Eu (TODGA)4(NO3)3 and Am(TODGA)4(NO3)3 from 1 M HNO3 with the stoichiometry decreasing as the HNO3 concentration increases. This result is supported by Arisaka and Kimura who investigated the speciation of Eu3+ in n-dodecane organic phases post-extraction by various concentrations of TODGA from multiple HNO3 concentrations using TRLFS.[31] The results clearly showed that in the organic phase the coordination sphere of Eu3+ does not contain any H2O and is, therefore, dominated by TODGA or NO3 − after extraction from all HNO3 concentrations.

Based on ODdDGA, the “Single-cycle method for partitioning of Minor Actinide using completely incinerable ReagenTs” (SMART) system was developed.37 This system is able to separate Am(III) from HLLW in a single processing cycle using a solvent of 0.05 mol·L−1 ODdDGA in n-dodecane, to which was added 0.2 mol·L−1 N,N-di-2-ethylhexyldiglycolamic acid (HDEHDGA) to improve the An−Ln separation. A new DGA extractant containing both isopropyl and dodecyl alkyl chains, iPDdDGA, was investigated for its extraction properties with the AmSel system, employing TODGA, as a reference. It was observed that iPDdDGA was similar to TODGA and other DGAs in kinetics and complex stoichiometry, but the distribution ratios are at least 1 order of magnitude larger than those observed for TODGA. Despite stronger extraction of trivalent actinides and lanthanides, selective stripping of Am is feasible with an aqueous phase containing 2.5 mmol·L−1 of SO3-Ph-BTBP in 0.1 mol·L−1 of HNO3, resulting in a SF Cm/Am of 2.8 and a SFLa/Am of 5. These values can be further optimized as separation factors between Cm and Am of 3.0 were readily obtained, and separation factors between La and Am as high as 19 were achieved. The SFCm/Am in the iPDdDGA-based AmSel was on average higher than the original TODGA-based AmSel process by a value of 0.5, a 20% improvement in separation factor.

The results showed that TrODGA has a similar stability behavior against radiolysis to TODGA. The weakest bond of TrODGA is C–Oether resulting in the formation of 2-hydroxy-Noctylacetamide as the main product. Metal complexation during irradiation indicates a protective effect where no change in the principal degradation pathway was observed. Evaluation of the TrODGA degraded samples revealed a reduction in the extraction efficiency of lanthanides and actinides and an undesirable coextraction increase of fission products, which could be attributed to the extraction properties of its degradation compounds. This study demonstrates the importance of thoroughly studying the effects of TrODGA and its degradation compounds for the applicability of TODGA-based solvents and the design of washing strategies to recycle them as much as possible.

N,N,N′,N′-Tetraoctyl diglycolamide (TODGA) is one of the most promising extractants tailored for high-level liquid radioactive waste treatment during nuclear fuel reprocessing. The γ-radiolysis of TODGA (0.2 mol/L) in n-dodecane (nDD) solution with and without pre-equilibrated 3.0 mol/L HNO3 was investigated using HPLC and UPLC-QTOF-MS and compared with the γ-radiolysis of neat TODGA in this study. With increased absorbed doses, the concentration of TODGA decreased exponentially for the studied systems. Moreover, pre-equilibration with HNO3 (3.0 mol/L) slightly influenced the γ-radiolysis of TODGA in nDD. Seven radiolytic products generated from the rupture of the C–C, C–O, and C–N bonds in TODGA were identified in the studied extraction system. The influence of γ-radiation on TODGA/nDD for the extraction of Eu(III) was evaluated using the first combination of extraction experiments and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, in which the complexations of Eu(III) with TODGA and its radiolytic products were systematically compared. Based on the radiolysis kinetic model of TODGA, the slope curve of the distribution ratio of Eu(III) ( D Eu ) and the absorbed dose, and fluorescence titration analysis, the empirical equation of the absorbed dose and D Eu was obtained successfully. Below 300 kGy, the experimental D Eu agreed well with the obtained empirical equation for TODGA/nDD. Conversely, at a high absorbed dose, the experimental D Eu was higher than the theoretical D Eu based on the empirical equation because the radiolytic products of TODGA with similar coordination structures still possessed partial complexation toward Eu(III), which was confirmed by DFT calculations. This work provides a method to predict the extraction distribution ratio of an irradiated extractant system and to understand the complex extraction process.

N,N,N′-Trioctyldiglycolamide (TrODGA) is an important degradation compound of N,N,N′,N′-tetraoctyl diglycolamide (TODGA), one of the most successful extractants under study for minor actinide recovery. Due to the lipophobicity and structural similarity to TODGA, easy removal of this degradation compound is not expected. Once formed, TrODGA can be accumulated or broken into smaller degradation compounds during process operation. In this study, the stability of TrODGA and its possible implications on TODGA-solvents were evaluated by studying its resistance to radiation, its contribution to the formation of degradation compounds, and the consequent extraction behavior. For that, relevant experimental conditions of the process operation, such as the presence of nitric acid and metals, were chosen. TrODGA samples pre-equilibrated with a solution containing the nitric acid and Eu concentration expected in a real process were subjected to gamma radiation. The results showed that TrODGA has a similar stability behavior against radiolysis to TODGA. The weakest bond of TrODGA is C–Oether resulting in the formation of 2-hydroxy-N-octylacetamide as the main product. Metal complexation during irradiation indicates a protective effect where no change in the principal degradation pathway was observed. Evaluation of the TrODGA degraded samples revealed a reduction in the extraction efficiency of lanthanides and actinides and an undesirable coextraction increase of fission products, which could be attributed to the extraction properties of its degradation compounds. This study demonstrates the importance of thoroughly studying the effects of TrODGA and its degradation compounds for the applicability of TODGA-based solvents and the design of washing strategies to recycle them as much as possible.

One of the research topics in many countries is to innovate the nuclear fuel cycle with the aim of reducing the long-term radiotoxicity and heat load of their nuclear waste. In this regard, many efforts have been undertaken in recent decades with the objective of developing pyro- and hydrometallurgical separation processes for the recovery of valuable elements (such as U and Pu), as well as important contributors to long-term radiotoxicity like minor actinides (MA: Np–Cm).1–6 Within the hydrometallurgical option, based on liquid–liquid extraction, diglycolamides (DGAs) are well-known as efficient extractants of trivalent actinides and lanthanides (An(iii) and Ln(iii)), through the formation of the corresponding complexes. Moreover, these compounds adhere to the CHON principle, making them entirely incinerable and thus minimizing the generation of secondary waste. In particular, the most popular of this ligand family is TODGA (N,N,N′,N′-tetraoctyldiglycolamide as shown in Fig. 1).7–11 TODGA is being extensively studied for various separation processes within the European strategy, such as the i-SANEX,12–14 EURO-EXAM,15 AmSel,16 and EURO-GANEX17–23 processes.

An important requirement for hydrometallurgical separation processes is that the selective extractants employed should be resistant to the harsh conditions they are exposed to during the process, i.e., a high radiation field and high nitric acid concentrations where the spent nuclear fuel is dissolved.24 These conditions induce the formation of radicals of the diluents that can cause a radiolysis-induced breakdown of the ligands, producing degradation compounds (DCs), and consequently, increasing the amount of secondary waste.24–27 Some of these DCs might exhibit extraction properties similar to the initial ligand, while others could have properties that significantly deviate from the original performance of the system. This divergence could lead to a substantial reduction in process efficiency. Additionally, these compounds have the potential to alter the hydrodynamic behavior of the process.28,29 In an industrial-scale scenario, the hydrometallurgical partitioning process operates continuously with an emphasis on reusing the organic phase as much as possible. Therefore, to maintain an optimal performance, it becomes necessary to implement a washing strategy targeting the most detrimental DCs. Particularly, in the case of DGA solvents, these washing steps are still under development. Some of these DCs could potentially be removed through conventional acidic or basic washing procedures, but the majority of them are likely to be accumulated in the organic solvent due to its lipophobic nature. But also, there will be an equilibrium between their formation and their subsequent own degradation, resulting in the generation of secondary DCs, and complicating the composition of the solvent even more. Therefore, to effectively design a hydrometallurgical process and to ensure safe long-term performance of the extraction process, a comprehensive understanding and predictive capability of the radiolytic degradation of the employed solvents are crucial.

With the aim of better understanding TODGA degradation, numerous studies have been conducted in the last few decades to investigate the stability of this ligand against the aforementioned conditions. Several authors have carried out radiolytic and hydrolytic stability studies of TODGA under different conditions. These studies have led to the conclusion that TODGA exhibits sufficient resistance for process operation until significantly absorbed doses.30–42 Nevertheless, this does not change the fact that TODGA undergoes degradation as a consequence of radiolysis resulting in the production of several DCs. The most abundant of these compounds arise from the cleavage of the C−Oether and Ccarbonyl–N bonds.31,35–40 Scarce studies have been published concerning the extraction properties of these DCs.43–46 In one of them, we assessed the different extraction and back-extraction properties of most of them along the different steps of the i-SANEX process. This assessment revealed that a few DCs have similar properties to TODGA, but other DCs extract undesirable elements like fission products (FPs), potentially leading to a reduction in process efficiency.43

Owing to their abundance and extraction properties, the most important TODGA DCs are N,N-dioctyl-2-hydroxyacetamide (DOHyA), N-octylacetamide (DOAA) and N,N,N′-trioctyldiglycolamide (TrODGA), whose structures are depicted in Fig. 1. Due to the chemical similarity between TrODGA and TODGA, it is expected that this DC will not be easily removed during the washing step and therefore may accumulate in the degraded TODGA solvent. Consequently, a better understanding of its extraction properties and radiolysis is crucial for further comprehension and prediction of TODGA degraded solvents’ behaviour. In one of our previous studies, the TrODGA solvent was observed to have good extraction properties for Lns, but also an undesirable coextraction of some FPs was observed, which could affect considerably the performance of TODGA solvents. This issue was addressed by implementing a scrubbing step at a lower nitric acid concentration, effectively resolving the concern.43 However, concerning the TrODGA washing strategy and radiolysis, to our knowledge, there are no studies dealing with these issues. Therefore, the goal of this work is twofold: first, to delve into the stability of this critical TODGA degradation compound and ascertain its contribution to the accumulation of DCs in a TODGA solvent during operation; second, to identify and quantify the TrODGA DCs—a task that has not been previously performed. This will lead to a better understanding of the composition and the impact of not only the irradiated TrODGA solvents but also TODGA-based solvents. Additionally, for a better prediction of the long-term solvent behavior, this study incorporates realistic conditions, such as the influence of metal complexation on ligand stability, since this is one of the major factors affecting the ligand environment. In this sense, after γ-irradiation of TrODGA samples under different conditions (with and without the presence of europium), changes in the solvent composition and the extraction performance were evaluated by HPLC-MS, gamma spectrometry and ICP-MS.

N,N,N′-Trioctyldiglycolamide (TrODGA) was synthesized as described in the literature.31 The organic phase diluent, consisting of (95 : 5)%vol odorless kerosene (OK)/1-octanol was chosen based on the solubility of TrODGA. OK and 1-octanol were purchased from Alfa Aesar (Germany) and Merck (Germany), respectively. The degradation compounds employed for calibration curves in the quantification analysis were synthesized in accordance with procedures detailed in previous studies.31,47 Nitric acid, used as an aqueous diluent, was purchased from VWR Chemical as 65% nitric acid (HNO3) with AnalaR NORMAPUR grade for analysis. It was additionally purified twice by a Quartz sub boiling distillation system (MLS-Milestone) and used within a short period of time. The solutions of HNO3 were prepared by diluting concentrated nitric acid with ultrapure water. Eu(NO3)3·5H2O with a purity of 99.9% was procured from Aldrich (Germany). The radioactive tracer solutions of 241Am(iii) and 152Eu(iii) were obtained as MCl3, in HCl 1 mol L−1, from Isotope Products Laboratories, California (USA). To simulate the high-level waste stream after reprocessing typical spent fuels, a highly active raffinate (HAR) solution was employed. This solution was prepared using a specific dissolution strategy, primarily involving the use of metal nitrate salts.35 The detailed composition is provided in the solvent extraction performance section (also refers to Table 1). For the HPLC-MS measurements, the employed diluents were MeOH, CH3CN and HCOOH. MeOH and CH3CN with grade LC-MS were supplied by J. T. Baker, while HCOOH HiPerSolv CHROMANORM® with a ≥99% of purity was purchased from VWR Chemicals.

Irradiation experiments were performed at the Náyade irradiation facility (CIEMAT).48 This facility consists of a pool of 1.2 m in length by 4.5 m in depth that uses water as the biological shield. At the bottom of the pool, 60 sources of 60Co (each with a 15 mm diam. × 135 mm long) with a total activity of 1.1 × 1014 Bq can be distributed in six lots. The cylindrical irradiation container used ensures a homogeneous irradiation flux within a 60 mm diam. × 100 mm high volume.

Different samples of TrODGA (0.05 mol L−1 in (95 : 5)%vol OK/1-octanol), pre-equilibrated with 3 mol L−1 HNO3, were irradiated in steps of 50 kGy up to 500 kGy. These irradiation experiments were conducted at a dose rate of 9.23 kGy h−1, as determined by Fricke dosimetry.49 To assess the effect of the metal in the degradation of this compound, samples of TrODGA after extraction with 17 mmol L−1 Eu(NO3)3·5H2O in 3 mol L−1 HNO3 were subjected to gamma radiation at the same absorbed doses using an identical dose rate, to compare both experiments. Extraction experiments using the irradiated organic phases were performed immediately after the last irradiation step. Reference samples were kept in the laboratory during the irradiation process for control.

Extraction experiments were performed using 0.5 mL of fresh and some irradiated organic phases (0.05 mol L−1 TrODGA in OK) and 0.5 mL of fresh aqueous phase with 3 mol L−1 HNO3 containing 10%vol of a simulated HAR solution in 4 mol L−1 HNO3 (concentrations are listed in Table 1) and spiked with 10 μL of 241Am(iii) and 152Eu(iii) in 0.5 mol L−1 HNO3 (100 kBq mL−1 each). The HAR solution corresponds to a high-level waste solution coming from UOX fuel reprocessing, with an initial 235U enrichment of 3.5%, a thermal burn-up of 33 GWd per tHM and 3 years of cooling,35 the composition of which is shown in Table 1. All extraction experiments were performed by mixing both the aqueous and organic phases, for 30 min at room temperature (22 ± 2 °C). Then, the phases were separated by centrifugation (5 min at 5000 rpm) and aliquots of each were taken for analysis (300 μL for gamma and 100 μL for ICP-MS).

For high energy gamma spectroscopy measurements, a high-purity-germanium detector with an intrinsic efficiency of 20% was used, using Genie-2000 (Canberra) as analysis software. The gamma characteristic photopeaks at 59.5 keV and 121.8 keV were analyzed for 241Am and 152Eu, respectively. The concentration of the elements presented in the HAR solution was determined using an ICP-MS, which is a Thermo Fischer Scientific iCAPTM Q apparatus using the collision cell technology mode. The organic and aqueous phases were analyzed directly after a suitable dilution with HNO3 (2–5%). All extraction results are reported as distribution ratio D (DM = [M]org/[M]aq), where a DM between 0.01 and 100 exhibits a maximum error of ±5%.

The chemical composition of the irradiated organic samples was examined by HPLC-MS. HPLC measurements were performed by using a Bruker EVOQTM HPLC-MS (Triple Quadrupole detector). An ACE 3 C18-PFP column (50 mm × 2.1 mm) was employed and maintained at 40 °C. The analysis involved utilizing a mobile phase gradient comprising two components: A: 0.1% HCOOH in H2O, and B: 0.1% HCOOH in CH3CN. The ESI+ ionization mode was used for the quantification of TrODGA and its DCs. Samples for the HPLC-MS studies were analyzed without pre-evaporation and diluted 1 : 30 000 in HPLC grade MeOH. Calibration curves were performed for TrODGA and the TrODGA DCs, from 10 to 1000 ppb, and the correlation coefficients in all cases were in the range of 0.996–0.999. All measurements were performed in duplicate to have uncertainty analysis where results show a maximum error of ±4%.

In this study, 0.05 mol L−1 TrODGA in (95 : 5)%vol OK/1-octanol pre-equilibrated with 3 mol L−1 HNO3 was irradiated up to 500 kGy. A slight color change from slightly yellow to dark yellow was found during the irradiation process, but no third-phase formation, precipitation, or other physical changes were observed, as could be expected from the experience gained from TODGA ligand studies.31,38–40

After the irradiation process, the composition of the degraded solvent (degradation of the main ligand and consequently the formation of its DCs) was analyzed by HPLC-MS. This analytical approach aimed to comprehend the degradation pathways and identify the specific degradation compounds that emerged. The ultimate goal was to evaluate the behavior of the irradiated solvent under these conditions.

To evaluate the degradation of TrODGA, first, a qualitative HPLC-MS analysis of the reference and the irradiated sample (at 500 kGy) of TrODGA was performed and the results are presented in Fig. 2. On the one hand, in the reference system (TrODGA pre-equilibrated with 3 mol L−1 HNO3 at 0 kGy), different signals were detected. These signals not only correspond to TrODGA, but also to other ones related to its hydrolysis products, such as octylamine (DC VI) and DC IX (structures shown in Fig. 3). The presence of 1-octanol in the diluent provides a higher polarity of the diluent which also results in a higher extraction of nitric acid from the aqueous into the organic phase. As a consequence, the nitric acid contained in the organic phase could favor a hydrolysis reaction of the amide group, producing hydrolysis products.

On the other hand, the results observed for the TrODGA system irradiated up to 500 kGy show an intensity decrease in the TrODGA signal as a consequence of degradation, and the presence of several DCs, most of which are typical of TODGA DCs (I, II, III, IV, V, VI, VII, VIII, IX).31–33,36–40,43 The peak at m/z 357.3, corresponding to the loss of an octyl group, can potentially be attributed to the formation of two distinct isomers depending on which specific amide the cleavage occurs. According to the retention time and mass profile, the structure depicted in Fig. 3 as DC VIII can be assigned to this mass. This deduction indicates that the dealkylation reaction predominantly occurs within the secondary amide. Besides these DCs, another signal corresponding to a new unidentified DC (m/z = 256.4, r.t. = 4.04 min) was detected (represented as a star in Fig. 2). This signal is related to a hypothetical TODGA DC which was proposed in our previous papers37,38 and recently suggested by Kimberlin et al.32 The chemical structures of the aforementioned DCs are presented in Fig. 3.

With the aim of analyzing the degradation of TrODGA due to gamma radiation, quantitative HPLC-MS measurements of TrODGA and its DCs were also carried out. The quantification of TrODGA as a function of absorbed dose is presented in Fig. 4. As expected, the TrODGA concentration diminishes with increasing dose. The data representing the concentration change against absorbed dose aligns well with an exponential curve (C = C0e−k′dos), suggesting a pattern of pseudo-first-order kinetics. This phenomenon is likely attributed to its reaction with a direct radiolysis product of the diluent. By means of the exponential fit, the dose constant k′ was determined, serving as an indicator of the overall stability of the extractant, independently of their concentration in solution. The results displayed in Fig. 4 yield a k′ value of (0.0036 ± 0.0004) kGy−1 for TrODGA samples. Since G-values have historically been reported in different studies of systems undergoing radiolysis, this value has been calculated using the equation G0 = d·C0/ρ from the literature.27,33 The obtained result is (−0.22 ± 0.02) μmol J−1. These values (k′ and G0) are consistent with literature findings for TODGA solvents,30,32,40,50,51 which is reasonable, given the structural chemical similarity between TODGA and TrODGA. Therefore, this compound seems to have a similar stability behavior against radiolysis as TODGA.

Taking into account the qualitative results obtained from the HPLC-MS analysis, the most important identified TrODGA DCs (I–VIII), because of their concentration or known extraction properties, were quantified at the different absorbed doses studied (Fig. 5). As a function of the dose, first, an increase in the concentrations of most quantified TrODGA DCs upon irradiation up to 250 kGy can be observed, followed by a decrease or plateau and then a further increase in most degradation products. This result highlights the phenomenon of secondary degradation as a result of ‘mother ligand’ radiolysis. It means, in a TODGA solvent, in turn, TrODGA will be degraded upon formation, resulting in an equilibrium between both phenomena. In fact, a semiquantitative study performed by Zarzana et al. pointed to this equilibrium situation.40 This effect is also observed for some of the DCs formed in TrODGA irradiated samples at 250–300 kGy. Their concentrations decrease and then continue to increase as a consequence of the degradation of the initial ligand, TrODGA. Considering that TrODGA has a similar chemical structure as TODGA, the weakest bonds are expected to be C–Oether and C–N, as has been already demonstrated in different studies.31,35–40 As TrODGA is an asymmetric DGA, depending on which of the two different C–Oether bonds are broken, the main compounds could be DC IV and DC III, or DC V and DC VII. In this study, the most abundant DCs are DC IV (N,N-dioctyl-acetamide) and DC III (2-hydroxy-N-octylacetamide) in 36% and 19%, respectively, with respect to initial TrODGA (data for 500 kGy), (see Table 2) indicating that the first pathway is preferred. The DC production has been compared with different radiolysis studies of TODGA52 and TrODGA (Table 2). It can be concluded that DC IV is mainly produced by the degradation of TrODGA, since in the case of TODGA, only 4% is formed, meanwhile DOHyA and TrODGA are the most abundant (29 and 12%, respectively). In addition to the products generated by the rupture of the ether bond, other DCs were observed such as DC VIII, which is the product of a dealkylation reaction and usually is not detected in the degradation of TODGA at 500 kGy (double dealkylation from TODGA). This evidences that in a degraded TODGA solvent where TrODGA will be continually formed, due to its radiolysis, the formation of these compounds (mostly DC IV and DC VIII) could be significant. Their extraction properties could have an important impact on the process performance. Therefore, this study reveals the importance of these DCs, and deep studies should be performed with the objective to assess their implications.

In view of the reduction in the TrODGA concentration and the DC quantification results, the obtained overall quantity almost corresponds with the disappeared TrODGA concentration. The remaining amount is ascribed to unquantified DCs such as the hypothetical structure shown in Fig. 3. This concludes that the TrODGA degradation practically produces all the identified and quantified DCs.

After the analysis of the composition of TrODGA degraded solvents, an evaluation of the extraction behavior was performed in order to gauge the impact of the DCs on the extraction performance. For that, an extraction experiment was carried out using the irradiated organic phase and a fresh aqueous phase containing a 10%vol HAR solution (composition in Table 1) in 3 mol L−1 HNO3 and spiked with 241Am and 152Eu, as representative elements of actinides and lanthanides, respectively. During the extraction operation, any precipitate or third phase formation were observed indicating a normal phase separation behavior. The concentration of Am, Eu and all other elements present in the HAR solution was determined in both the organic and the aqueous phases. The results, represented as distribution ratios, are shown in Fig. 6 for the non-irradiated and irradiated samples. For the unirradiated sample (0 kGy), only the heavy lanthanides (Sm, Eu (analyzed with two techniques) and Gd) are well extracted (DM > 1), while the light lanthanides (La, Ce, Pr and Nd) and the actinide (Am) show lower extraction, except for Y, which shows a high distribution ratio (DM > 10). The extraction of the heavy lanthanides agrees with previous results.43 For the irradiated samples, in general, the radiolytic degradation of the extractant gives rise to a reduction in the different D values as a function of the absorbed dose. For instance, at an absorbed dose of 500 kGy, for lanthanides and actinide elements, the D values are <1. Concerning the fission products (at 0 kGy), it is important to note the original low affinity for these elements (showing DM < 1). However, the extraction of some fission products such as Mo and Sn increased as a function of the absorbed dose. This highlights the importance of TrODGA DCs, of which some of them could have a high affinity for these elements increasing the D values and may negatively influence the performance of the process. Previous studies have shown that 2-hydroxy-N-octylacetamide (DC IV) has an affinity for fission products such as Mo, Pd and Zr.43 In fact, N,N-dioctyl-2-hydroxyacetamide (DOHyA; DC VII), as well as some of its DCs, extracts some FPs such as Mo,46 which is in perfect agreement with the present results. Therefore, this result highlights that for the prediction of a TODGA-based solvent, DC IV and DC VII should be formed as a consequence of not only TODGA degradation but also one of its major DCs, TrODGA. These results also indicate that the use of an FP masking agent should be checked also in the presence of this kind of DCs to ensure that they are able to avoid the extraction of undesirable elements such as Mo, Zr and Pd.53

During the extraction process, the organic phase composed of TODGA and its DCs is in contact with an aqueous phase containing different metals, which may give rise to formation of complexes. Therefore, for a better prediction of the long-term solvent behavior, more realistic irradiation experiments were performed taking into account the effect of the metal complexation on the stability of TrODGA. Eu(iii) was selected for these studies, since it is a lanthanide present in a real feed of the processes to recover the MA. Moreover, it forms complexes with TrODGA and enables the work with radioactive and non-radioactive solutions.

To study the nature of the complex formed by TrODGA with Eu(iii), the extraction of Eu(iii) as a tracer was carried out as a function of the TrODGA concentration maintaining constant the HNO3 concentration. Fig. 7 shows the extraction of Eu(iii) from 3 mol L−1 HNO3 into an organic phase with varying concentrations of TrODGA dissolved in (95 : 5)%vol OK/1-octanol. It exhibits that upon adding increasing concentrations of TrODGA, DEu(iii) increases. The slope of the linear fit of the logarithmic distribution ratio as a function of the logarithmic ligand concentration provides a slope of (3.11 ± 0.09) for 152Eu. This value implies that there are three molecules of TrODGA involved in the formed complex during the extraction process. The 3 : 1 (ligand : Eu) complex formation isconsistent with the findings observed for TODGA and Eu9,54 and other elements.7 This concordance is logical, given that TrODGA has a similar chemical structure to TODGA, implying the occurrence of complexation at analogous structural sites.

These complexes may have different implications on the ligand's stability. Therefore, the extraction with 0.05 mol L−1 TrODGA in (95 : 5)%vol OK/1-octanol with 17 mmol L−1 Eu(NO3)3·5H2O in 3 mol L−1 HNO3 was carried out at the same extraction conditions described before and the loaded organic phase was irradiated at the same absorbed doses. The selected europium concentration was chosen for an easy understanding of the metal effect on the stability of the ligand, since all molecules of TrODGA should be involved in the complex taking into account the stoichiometry described above.

The results of the qualitative HPLC-MS analysis of the reference and the most irradiated sample (500 kGy) of TrODGA are presented in Fig. 8. For the unirradiated situation, similar peaks are observed as without the metal, but also a signal corresponding to m/z = 372.4. This signal can be attributed to a secondary reaction of DC IX with MeOH, during the HPLC-MS sample preparation, forming methyl 2-(2-(dioctylamino)-2-oxoethoxy)acetate, which is also favored by the presence of a Lewis acid like europium cation. In the irradiated sample, the identified DCs are identical to the ones formed in the previous case, i.e., when irradiating TrODGA solutions after contact with nitric acid and without metals. However, a difference in the peak intensities abundance of the ions in the different samples was measured. An increase in the signals of DC I, DC VI and the proposed hypothetical DC (marked as a star in the chromatogram) was found. However, there is a reduction in the intensities of the signals corresponding to DC III and V (Fig. 2). The peak corresponding to TrODGA in the irradiated sample has a higher intensity than in the previous case. These results indicate a higher stability of TrODGA upon complexation with Eu ions.

To corroborate the previous observation, the quantification of TrODGA as well as its DCs was performed. The concentration of TrODGA as a function of the absorbed dose after extraction with Eu(NO3)3·5H2O in 3 mol L−1 HNO3 is shown in Fig. 9. As expected, the TrODGA concentration decreases as a function of dose. However, in this case, the dose constant k′ is (0.0015 ± 0.0004) kGy−1 and the G-value is (0.09 ± 0.02) μmol J−1, which are lower than those obtained in the absence of metals (see Fig. 4), indicating the higher stability of TrODGA against radiation when it is complexed with Eu. This increase in the stability of the ligand is in agreement with different studies in the literature: DOHyA as well as TODGA were irradiated in the presence of europium and an increase in stability was observed.46,52 Another study was carried out by Kimberlin et al., who concluded that TODGA was more stable when irradiated in the presence of Nd ions.33 This phenomenon can be explained by the protective effect due to the complexation, increasing the stability of the ligand, in this case, TrODGA.

Regarding the TrODGA radiolysis pathways in the presence of europium, Fig. 10 summarizes the quantitative results obtained for its DCs as a function of absorbed dose. As expected, the concentration of the DCs increases as a function of the absorbed dose due to the degradation of TrODGA. There are no appreciable differences in the most abundant DCs compared to the experiment in the absence of a metal. However, it is important to note that the amount of the DCs is lower, corresponding with less degradation when metal complexation takes place during the irradiation process (see Table 3). As occurs in the case without metal, for some DCs, the concentration decreases approximately at 300 kGy, and then increases, which points that these radiolysis products themselves are susceptible to radiolysis. From these quantification results, it can be concluded that the sum of these quantified DCs corresponds to the amount of TrODGA degradation, as in the earlier case. It demonstrates that the TrODGA degradation can be explained mostly with the analyzed and quantified DCs in this work.

N,N,N′-Trioctyldiglycolamide (TrODGA) is an important and abundant degradation compound formed because of the radiolytic degradation of the well-known diglycolamide TODGA extractant. Since it may have an impact on the process performance due to its extraction properties and own radiolysis, the stability of TrODGA against gamma radiation was assessed individually by evaluating its degradation and its implication on the extraction performance considering the effect of metal complexation on ligand stability, as a better approach to realistic conditions.

With regard to structural degradation, the presence of nine DCs was always observed, the rupture of the C–Oether bond being predominant. Since TrODGA is an asymmetric DGA, the main compounds could be DC IV and DC III, or DC V and DC VII, depending on which C–Oether bond is broken. From the results, it can be concluded that the most abundant DCs are DC IV and DC III, indicating this as the preference for the cleavage of the corresponding C–Oether bond. In addition, the loss of one octyl group could generate two different isomers. However, the findings support the conclusion that the dealkylation reaction predominantly occurs in the secondary amide, resulting in the formation of DC VIII.

Concerning the extraction properties, the radiolytic degradation of TrODGA gives rise to a reduction in the different lanthanide D values as a function of the absorbed dose. However, the extraction of some fission products such as Mo and Sn increased for the same samples. This behavior can be explained by the formation of DC IV and DC VII, which have a higher affinity for these elements.

In conclusion, the findings of this study not only enrich the understanding of TODGA degradation pathways, attributable to TrODGA's asymmetry, but also highlight the significance of accumulating degradation compounds (DCs) due to secondary degradation in TODGA-based solvents. Until now, all TODGA stability studies have emphasized the significance of DOHyA and DOAA (identified as DC VII and DC III in this work) to design the recycling strategies of the solvent. However, this research also points out the importance of 2-hydroxy-N-octylacetamide (DC IV), which is obtained from TODGA and mainly other DCs such as TrODGA and DOHyA. Further studies are necessary to elucidate better its accumulation and extraction properties. This will be important to predict the process performance of a degraded TODGA solvent with the aim of planning proper washing strategies to recycle it.

The effect of the alkyl substituents on amidic N atoms in diglycolamide (DGA) compounds on solvent extraction has been investigated. The solubility in water and n-dodecane, lanthanide loading capacity, and distribution ratios (D) of lanthanides and actinides for various DGA compounds are reported. DGA derivatives with short alkyl chains, for example, methyl and ethyl groups, are very water soluble, while DGA derivatives with long alkyl chains, for example, octyl (TODGA), decyl (TDDGA), dodecyl (TDdDGA), and 2-ethylhexyl (TEHDGA) group are moderately soluble in n-dodecane. DGA derivatives with phenyl substituents have very low solubility in both aqueous and organic solvents, which suggests that these compounds will not be suitable for solvent extraction applications in the HNO3/n-dodecane systems. The lanthanide loading capacities of DGA extractants correlate with their alkyl chain lengths according to the following order: TDdDGA > TDDGA > TODGA > TEHDGA. The branched-alkyl-chain DGA derivative (TEHDGA) exhibits both lower D and loading capacity than TODGA. The results of masking-effect and solubility tests indicate that TEDGA is the best actinide masking agent among the water-soluble DGA derivatives tested. Actinide and lanthanide extractions using ten DGA compounds in six diluents (nitrobenzene, 1,2-dichloroethane, 1-octanol, chloroform, toluene, and n-dodecane) are also reported; it was observed that lipophilic DGA derivatives with shorter alkyl chains show higher D values.

Diglycolamide (DGA) derivatives, a promising class of extractants for the recovery of minor actinides from HNO3, were introduced by Stephan and co-workers in 1991. When toluene was used as a diluent, N,N’-dimethyl-N,N’-dihexyl(DMDH)-DGA achieved high distribution ratios for divalent and trivalent metals for extraction from HCl media. Choppin and co-workers reported on the extraction of lanthanides (Ln) and actinides (An) using DMDHDGA as an adduct in a study of synergistic extraction with thenoyltrifluoroacetone (TTA). The DMDHDGA used in these reports has limited solubility in n-dodecane. To remedy this limitation, Sasaki et al. synthesized N,N,N’,N’-tetraoctyl-diglycolamide (TODGA), which exhibits good lipophilicity and moderate solubility in n-dodecane. These authors demonstrated the use of TODGA as the extractant for a broad selection of metals including actinides. Several reports have established that TODGA can be used as an extractant for the recovery of minor actinides from high-level radioactive liquid waste (HLW), and both fundamental studies and applications-development work using DGA compounds have been reported. Though several DGA compounds have been studied worldwide, the effect of alkyl substituents on extractability has not been examined in detail.

One of the most important factors in developing novel extractants is the selection of suitable functional groups. Among the class of solvating extractants, diamides, phosphonates, phosphinates, and phosphine oxides with different secondary functional groups have been reported.[Citation15−Citation23] These studies have principally focused on measuring their distribution ratios, loading capacities, and basicity. The alkyl groups attached to the amide functional groups in these investigations appear generally to have limited effect on metal ion extractability. However, it is well-known from the prior literature that the lipophilic component of an extractant does generally impact system performance. The purpose of this study is to examine the effect of alkyl substituents on Ln and An extraction; DGA compounds exhibit strong affinity for nitrate salts of Ln and An. Figure 1 shows the structures of the twelve DGA derivatives used in this study. The primary focus of the work is on developing an improved understanding of the influence of the amidic alkyl groups on DGA solubility in water and n-dodecane, lanthanide loading capacity, and their partitioning effectiveness (as indicated by D values).

Dissolution tests for the twelve DGA compounds in water and n-dodecane were done using 50 mg, samples or 50 μL of the liquid samples were added into 3 mL of water or n-dodecane. The sample solutions were shaken at 50°C and gently allowed to settle for one or two hours in the laboratory, and solubility evaluated at room temperature without centrifugation. Solubility in water was determined by TOC (TOC-V CSN, SHIMADZU), and that in n-dodecane was done by observation. The degree of dissolution was qualitatively divided into three categories (less soluble, unclear, and soluble) based on these observations.

Various concentrations (0.002–6.0 M) of HNO3 containing Ln and An metals and various concentrations (0.001–0.5 M) of DGA/n-dodecane after pre-equilibration were used as the aqueous and organic phases. The Ln and An metal concentrations used in solvent extraction were 100 ppb and sub-ppb level, respectively. Loading-capacity experiments were performed with 1–100 mM Nd in 3 M HNO3 and 0.1 M DGA/n-dodecane. Both phases (0.5–1.0 mL) were contacted and mechanically shaken at 1500 rpm for 50 min at 25°C using a thermo-regulated bath. The phase ratio of the aqueous and the organic phases is set normally to 1:1. After mixing with 5 mL of PICO–AQUA cocktail, the alpha activities of 241Am, 230Th, 237Np, 233U, and 238Pu in the aqueous and the organic phases were measured using a liquid scintillation counter (Tri-Carb 1600 TR, Packard Instrument Company). The normal oxidation states of An are employed, namely, Am(III), Th(IV), Pu(IV), Np(V), and U(VI). Pu(IV) can be prepared by electrolysis and checked by UV-visible spectrophotometry.[Citation25] To inhibit disproportionation to Pu(III) and Pu(VI), Pu(IV) is kept in 5 M HNO3. The beta activity of 233Pa generated from the decay of 237Np was removed weekly by solvent extraction using TODGA.[Citation26] The aqueous phase containing Ln metals was diluted with HNO3 and distilled water. The metal ion concentration in the aqueous phase was measured by an ICP-AES (SPS 3100, Seiko Instrument) or ICP-MS (SPQ 9200, Seiko Instrument) spectrometers. The metal ion concentration of the organic phase was determined by subtracting the aqueous metal concentrations from initial concentration. The D values, defined as the ratio of total metal concentration in the organic phase vs. that in the aqueous phase were calculated. The error for measurement of radioactivity is in the range of 2–20% (based on counting statistics for 30,000 count/30 min. for each experiment). Distribution ratios were generally maintained in the range of 0.001 to 1000. A similar degree of uncertainty can be considered for distribution ratios determined based on ICP-AES or ICP-MS measurements.

Simple observation tests after mixing DGA sample in n-dodecane and the determination of total organic carbon concentration in water by TOC were done. TMDGA, TDDGA, TDdDGA, DMDPDGA, and TPhDGA are solids at room temperature. TEDGA, TPDGA, TBDGA, TADGA, THDGA, and TODGA are viscous liquids. In general, high-molecular-weight organic compounds are expected to have high melting and boiling points; thus, it is not surprising that TEDGA, TPDGA, TBDGA, TADGA, THDGA, and TODGA are liquids, and that TDDGA and TDdDGA are solids at ambient temperature. TMDGA, DMDPDGA, and TPhDGA deviate from this trend, exhibiting unexpected miscibility properties. The two DGA derivatives containing phenyl groups, DMDPDGA and TPhDGA, are notably insoluble in both water and n-dodecane, likely attributable to the presence of the π-electrons. TMDGA, containing the shortest alkyl groups and thus the most compact DGA derivative in this study, likely exists in the solid state at room temperature as a result of favorable lattice energy for the solid. The solubilities of DGA compounds in water are: TMDGA and TEDGA, unlimited solubility; TPDGA, 57 mM; TBDGA, 2.3 mM; TADGA, 0.27 mM; THDGA, 0.11 mM; TODGA and TDDGA, 0.042 mM.[Citation5] TDdDGA, TEHDGA, DMDPDGA, and TPhDGA, have quite limited solubility in water.

The dissolution of DGA derivatives in n-dodecane was confirmed by observation. After mixing the solid samples (TMDGA, DMDPDGA, and TPhDGA) with n-dodecane using the protocol described above, residual solid remains at the bottom of the sample. These DGA compounds are characterized as “less soluble” in n-dodecane. For liquid samples of TEDGA and TPDGA, the solvents after mixing become cloudy; these samples are also characterized as “less soluble” in n-dodecane. Other samples show a clear solution after mixing, indicating solubility in n-dodecane. From the present work, the DGA derivatives with short alkyl chains (namely TMDGA and TEDGA) are highly water-soluble and exhibit low solubility in n-dodecane. The DGA derivatives with long alkyl chains (THDGA, TODGA, TDDGA, TDdDGA, and TEHDGA) have low solubility in water and moderate solubility in n-dodecane. TPDGA is soluble in both water and in n-dodecane. The aryl-diamide-DGA derivatives, DMDPDGA and TPhDGA, exhibit low solubility in both water and in n-dodecane. TPDGA, TBDGA, TADGA, and DMDPDGA, which have relatively low solubility in n-dodecane, are highly soluble in more polar solvents, like chlorinated and aromatic solvents. Various An and Ln extraction tests using these diluents were performed and the results compared. These results are summarized in Supplemental Table S1.

Loading capacities of THDGA, TODGA, TDDGA, TDdDGA, and TEHDGA were measured and are summarized in Fig. 2 and Table 1. The method to determine loading capacity has been reported previously.[Citation27,Citation28] Since THDGA forms a third phase during pre-equilibration with 3 M HNO3, its loading capacity is described as 0 in Table 1. With the exception of TEHDGA, loading capacity increases with alkyl chain length. The highest loading capacity can be seen in TDdDGA, which has the longest alkyl groups. DGA derivatives and metal-DGA complexes having long chains are stable in hydrocarbons. TEHDGA is the only DGA derivative in this study having a branched-alkyl chain; lower loading capacity than that of a normal-octyl analog suggests that the branching could result in steric hindrance in the metal-complex. This facet of DGA chemistry will be considered further in the discussion of trans-lanthanide extraction trend. The two best-known and widely studied DGA compounds, TODGA and TEHDGA, used for partitioning of High Level Wastes (HLW),[Citation29−Citation32] each have relatively low lanthanide loading capacities. The loading capacity of TODGA can be increased by adding a phase modifier like tributyl phosphate (TBP) or a monoamide into the extraction solvent.[Citation12,Citation27]

The masking effects of hydrophilic DGA derivatives (TMDGA, TEDGA, and TPDGA) on the extraction of Am and Pu were studied. Extractions of Am(III) and Pu(IV) were done using 0.2 M TODGA/n-dodecane and 0.24 M HNO3 with and without water-soluble DGA. Similar studies have been reported previously.[Citation33,Citation34] The D values are summarized in Table 2. Even at 1 mM, hydrophilic DGA derivatives (TMDGA, TEDGA, and TPDGA) exhibited reduced values for DAm and DPu compared to those seen in the absence of hydrophilic DGA (Do(Am) = 0.7; Do(Pu) = 1.9), indicating that hydrophilic DGA masks the extraction of these An. From the D values in Table 2, the masking-effectiveness trend was found to be TEDGA > TPDGA > TMDGA. Under the conditions of these experiments, suppression factors (Do/D) are larger for Pu(IV) than for Am(III). The TPDGA concentration decreases after mixing with the organic phase, probably because of its partial miscibility in the organic phase due to its relatively long alkyl chains (see Supplemental Table S1). Considering these results, it can be observed that TEDGA is the best masking agent of all DGA compounds used in this work.

The D values of An (Am(III), Th(IV), Np(V), and U(VI)) using 6 diluents (chloroform, CL; 1,2-dichloroethane, DCE; n-dodecane, DD; nitrobenzene, NB; 1-octanol, OC; toluene, TL) and 10 DGA compounds are shown in Figs. 3–6 (Fig. 3, Am; Fig. 4, Th; Fig. 5, Np; and Fig. 6, U). There are no results for TPDGA in n-dodecane, nitrobenzene, 1-octanol, and toluene; TBDGA in n-dodecane; and DMDPDGA and TPhDGA in n-dodecane, 1-octanol, and toluene. In each case, the absence of data is attributable to the low solubility of these DGA derivatives in the respective diluents. The relationship between D values and extractant concentration are shown in Figs. 3–6.

The slope values for extractant dependencies of TPDGA, TODGA, TDdDGA, TEHDGA, and DMDPDGA are summarized in Supplemental Tables S2–S6. It can be observed that the slope values for extractant dependencies of DGA derivatives in the polar diluents NB, DCE, and OC are lower (e.g., D(Am)avg = 2.1(± 0.2)) than those for CL (D(Am)avg = 2.5(± 0.2)), TL (D(Am)avg = 3.5(± 0.4)), and DD (D(Am)avg = 3.4(± 0.4)). In general, extractant dependencies are lower for Th(IV), Np(V), and U(VI) than for Am(III) (Supplemental Tables S2–S5). The highest M:L values are generally seen in toluene, and they typically decrease in the order Am(III) > Th(IV) > U(VI) > Np(V) (e.g., for TODGA, 3.79(± 0.2) (Am) > 2.77(± 0.01) (Th) > 2.39(± 0.01) (U) > 2.03(± 0.01)). The diluent dielectric constants are NB, 35.7; DCE, 10.45; OC, 10.34; CL, 4.9; TL, 2.24; and DD, 2.02, which correlates more or less qualitatively with the order of increasing extractant dependence that is observed, as has been reported previously.[Citation35] The tables suggest that metal:extractant ratios of 1:1 and 1:2 are stable in polar solvents, and metal:extractant ratios of 1:2, 1:3, and 1:4 are stable in nonpolar solvents. This simplified analysis assumes that the extractants do not aggregate, that the number of metal ions in each complex is 1, and that acid extraction does not have an effect on slopes. Since it is not known whether these conditions are met, determination of the M:L stoichiometry of the complexes must await more detailed equilibrium analysis. In most cases, the trend in D values for each solvent is NB > DCE > DD > OC > TL > CL, in good agreement with previously reported results.[Citation35]

The D values of different oxidation states of An can also be compared. Based on previous results, DGA extractants are likely to exhibit high D values in Am3+ and Th4+ system, and D(Th) are generally equal to or little higher than D(Am) values. The tridentate DGA shows significantly weaker affinity for actinyl ions, Np(V) and U(VI), due to the steric strain imposed by the planar bonding environment imposed by the equatorial coordination sites of the linear dioxocations. The extractability of different An by DGA is Th(IV) ≥ Am(III) > U(VI) > Np(V), which is different from that of similar bidentate extractants, (An(IV) > An(VI) > An(III) > An(V)).[Citation36,Citation37]

Figure 7 illustrates the relationship between D(An) and the carbon numbers in the alkyl chain attached to the amidic N atoms of the DGA extractants. TEH and DMDP in the x-axis are obtained from TEHDGA and DMDPDGA, respectively; these extractants have substituents other than the normal-alkyl chain. Polar diluents, CL (Fig. 7a), DCE (Fig. 7b), and NB (Fig. 7c), each of which has a high solubility for all DGAs, were used in these experiments. It can be seen that the D values of TEHDGA and DMDPDGA (branched or asymmetric amides) are relatively low, compared to those of DGA derivatives that have straight-chained dialkyl amide groups. Extractability of carbamoyl phosphine oxide (CMPO) and diphosphine dioxide (DPDO) has been reported to increase because of their phenyl substituents, which have a unique aryl effect.[Citation38] Because these phenyl group of phosphine oxides have conjugated double bonds with P=O, these double bonds are more stable than a C–C single bond. However, the position of phenyl and carbonyl C=O groups in DGA derivatives (–CO–N–ph) is different from that of a conjugated double bond. The functional group, C=O, is less stable in aromatic amide derivatives of DGA because of the electron-withdrawing effect of phenyl groups.
Figure 7 also indicates that the D values decreased gradually with the number of carbon atoms in the alkyl chain. The D values for a smaller number of carbon atoms (3–6) decrease more noticeably than those for large numbers of carbon atoms (6–12). It is possible that the decrease in D values could correlate with steric hindrance; such an effect would likely be more significant in extractants with short carbon chains. The effect of carbon chain length on extractability, especially for mono- and diamides, have been studied.[Citation16,Citation39−Citation41] The results in the present work are in general agreement with those reported previously.

Figure 8 shows the relationships between D(Ln) and their atomic numbers. Studies of the trans-lanthanide pattern of extraction by TPDGA, TODGA, TDdDGA, TEHDGA, and DMDPDGA were completed in six solvents using 0.04–0.1 M DGA and 1 M HNO3. With the exception of the extractions of TPDGA into TL, DMDPDGA into CL and TL, and TEHDGA into 6 diluents, a gradual increase of D(Ln) with their atomic numbers is seen from La to approximately Ho, then a leveling off of extraction for Er–Lu. Greater difference of D values between light and heavy Ln can be observed for the conditions, TPDGA in NB, DCE, OC, or CL and TODGA and TDdDGA in NB, DCE, OC, and DD, in Figs. 8a-8c. Using these data, the mutual separation factors (SF), defined as D(Ln)/D(La), are summarized in Supplemental Tables S7–S11. It is seen that TODGA or TDdDGA dissolved in NB, OC, and DD give a SF(Lu/La) over 100 which suggests some potential utility of these DGA derivatives for lanthanide-specific separations. In contrast, DGA extractions into CL and TL give not only low D(Ln) values but also low SF(Ln/La). The Ln pattern of extraction is useful information in the context of the separation of An from Ln but also potentially of Am from Cm.[Citation42−Citation46] It can be seen from the present work that the diluents can have a profound effect on separation efficiency, though the effect cannot be fully understood from these observations. Previous reports have indicated that different extraction species occur in different diluents,[Citation35] and Yaita et al. showed the extracted species of monomer and dimer and M-TODGA with large number of nitrates.[Citation10] To explain the trans-Ln patterns seen in Fig. 8, the extracted species of Ln-TODGA must be studied in detail using a variety spectroscopic and thermochemical methods.
